entry.title: The Horseman’s Cafe
entry.content:
Banana smoothie
Macchiato (?)
Chicken and spinach filo
Stuffed kumara
Citrus tart
Melting moment

Horseman cafe is a Shannon institution, with The Fee-Rex remembering visiting 15 years ago. They continue to be popular, running a steady Sunday lunch trade which the kitchen seemed to be struggling with. “It’ll be 20 minutes for cooked food” they said. While we don’t mind a time warning, actually we appreciate it and adjusted our order accordingly, her tone was clear: “Don’t order any cooked food, I’m overwhelmed”.

Our cabinet fare came out erratically. We were given the biscuit at the counter. Pause. Next came the two heated savoury items. Pause. Later came a matcha. Yes, a matcha, not the macchiato we had ordered. More pause. Out comes the banana smoothie, and we finally question where our tart ended up. Turns out it had been forgotten, but does show up less than a minute after we enquired.

The only thing worth eating was the melting moment. It wasn’t groundbreaking, we’ve absolutely had better, but it was alright and we wouldn’t be sad to eat it again. The filo wasn’t overly enjoyable, the spinach was a bit fibrous, the capsicum chutney inside was over-sweet and the beetroot relish wasn’t a great accompaniment. The filo was a meh.

The other three things we ordered (and we’re not including the random matcha in this) were all bad. The stuffed kumara contained huge chunks of unmashed boiled kumara, the other contents were nondescript, and you had to seriously ration the paltry blobs of sour cream provided.

This was our first banana smoothie in a while, and it was a sad return for this BAATFR classic. The most obvious flaw was that this this beverage did not appear to contain any actual banana, which in our book means it does not meet the definition of a banana smoothie at all. There were sickly slicks of banana syrup decoratively cascaded down the inside of the cheap plastic cup. Bros, come on. We’re not huge fans of that technique at the best of times when honey is used, as it doesn’t actually add anything to the flavour of the drink. Doing this with your cheap nasty banana syrup is just offensive. Speaking of offensive, put your smoothie in a glass you lazy monkeys! This drink was served in one of those compostable “plastic” takeaway cold drink containers, sans straw. Out of a glass we don’t mind no straw, but today this was a problem because 1) you had to deal with the lip feel of this naff plastic, and 2) this thing was a foamy baby. The first mouthful was just bubbles, and the next few weren’t much better. As a final insult, she hadn’t even passed the temperature test, the whole thing was a tepid mess.

Finally we had the citrus tart. This looked alright in the cabinet, and a nice citrus tart is one of the world’s finest things. They’d even made their own pastry. Unfortunately, the pastry was not good at all. It was too thick and had a rubbery, cardboardy texture. They would have been better off purchasing a pre-made tart casing, at least then it would have been crisp. Lemon curd within a citrus tart should be citrusy and tart - it’s in the name! This was lacking in both departments, with the desired tang being replaced with a glum, sweet, lemon-adjacent goop.

The nicest thing we can say about The Horseman’s Cafe was that they had Country Life magazines available for customers. This is something different, and what a great read. As of 2025 there was a Scottish castle for sale, 20 bedroom, accessible only by ferry, and boy was it a fixer upper. But it was only 750,000 quid! Maybe we’ll take the profits from the immense commercial success of this restaurant review site and try our hand at castle restoration. Something to think about…
entry.summary: Banana smoothie Macchiato (?) Chicken and spinach filo Stuffed kumara Citrus tart Melting moment Horseman cafe is a Shannon institution, with The Fee-Rex remembering visiting 15 years ago. They continue to be popular, running a steady Sunday lunch trade which the kitchen seemed to be struggling with. “It’ll be 20 minutes for cooked food” they said. While we don’t mind a time warning, actually we appreciate it and adjusted our order accordingly, her tone was clear: “Don’t order any cooked food, I’m overwhelmed”. Our cabinet fare came out erratically. We were given the biscuit at the counter. Pause. Next came the two heated savoury items. Pause. Later came a matcha. Yes, a matcha, not the macchiato we had ordered. More pause. Out comes the banana smoothie, and we finally question where our tart ended up. Turns out it had been forgotten, but does show up less than a minute after we enquired. The only thing worth eating was the melting moment. It wasn’t groundbreaking, we’ve absolutely had better, but it was alright and we wouldn’t be sad to eat it again. The filo wasn’t overly enjoyable, the spinach was a bit fibrous, the capsicum chutney inside was over-sweet and the beetroot relish wasn’t a great accompaniment. The filo was a meh. The other three things we ordered (and we’re not including the random matcha in this) were all bad. The stuffed kumara contained huge chunks of unmashed boiled kumara, the other contents were nondescript, and you had to seriously ration the paltry blobs of sour cream provided. This was our first banana smoothie in a while, and it was a sad return for this BAATFR classic. The most obvious flaw was that this this beverage did not appear to contain any actual banana, which in our book means it does not meet the definition of a banana smoothie at all. There were sickly slicks of banana syrup decoratively cascaded down the inside of the cheap plastic cup. Bros, come on. We’re not huge fans of that technique at the best of times when honey is used, as it doesn’t actually add anything to the flavour of the drink. Doing this with your cheap nasty banana syrup is just offensive. Speaking of offensive, put your smoothie in a glass you lazy monkeys! This drink was served in one of those compostable “plastic” takeaway cold drink containers, sans straw. Out of a glass we don’t mind no straw, but today this was a problem because 1) you had to deal with the lip feel of this naff plastic, and 2) this thing was a foamy baby. The first mouthful was just bubbles, and the next few weren’t much better. As a final insult, she hadn’t even passed the temperature test, the whole thing was a tepid mess. Finally we had the citrus tart. This looked alright in the cabinet, and a nice citrus tart is one of the world’s finest things. They’d even made their own pastry. Unfortunately, the pastry was not good at all. It was too thick and had a rubbery, cardboardy texture. They would have been better off purchasing a pre-made tart casing, at least then it would have been crisp. Lemon curd within a citrus tart should be citrusy and tart - it’s in the name! This was lacking in both departments, with the desired tang being replaced with a glum, sweet, lemon-adjacent goop. The nicest thing we can say about The Horseman’s Cafe was that they had Country Life magazines available for customers. This is something different, and what a great read. As of 2025 there was a Scottish castle for sale, 20 bedroom, accessible only by ferry, and boy was it a fixer upper. But it was only 750,000 quid! Maybe we’ll take the profits from the immense commercial success of this restaurant review site and try our hand at castle restoration. Something to think about…

entry.title: Craggy Range
entry.content:
Bread with apple cider butter and honey
Raw lamb and beetroot tart
Confit duck pastry
Roast halloumi
Stuffed quail and potato
Leek and onion tart
Venison rack with black pudding
Brussel sprouts with roasted nuts
Garlic roast potatoes
Chocolate truffles

Sweet heavens Craggy range does nice food. We won’t spend too much time labouring each individual dish. There were a lot of them, the menu changes monthly, but it was 10s across the board. Particular shout out to the halloumi which was a chunky roasted block, which was a new and welcome way of enjoying this cheese for us. Oh and the bread, so soft yet with such a crunchy crust, the butter was really different, this kind of bread makes you wonder if you should be making bread at home, until you realise it would never taste like this.

While the meal was expensive, it was absolutely in line with the quality, and the servings were very generous. We weren’t chungus after this meal, but we certainly didn’t need any more food. In the spirit of nitpicking, the head on the espresso martinis was over-large, and the vodka espresso martini was too sweet. This was no dampener on what was an excellent dining experience.

Bookings recommended.
entry.summary: Bread with apple cider butter and honey Raw lamb and beetroot tart Confit duck pastry Roast halloumi Stuffed quail and potato Leek and onion tart Venison rack with black pudding Brussel sprouts with roasted nuts Garlic roast potatoes Chocolate truffles Sweet heavens Craggy range does nice food. We won’t spend too much time labouring each individual dish. There were a lot of them, the menu changes monthly, but it was 10s across the board. Particular shout out to the halloumi which was a chunky roasted block, which was a new and welcome way of enjoying this cheese for us. Oh and the bread, so soft yet with such a crunchy crust, the butter was really different, this kind of bread makes you wonder if you should be making bread at home, until you realise it would never taste like this. While the meal was expensive, it was absolutely in line with the quality, and the servings were very generous. We weren’t chungus after this meal, but we certainly didn’t need any more food. In the spirit of nitpicking, the head on the espresso martinis was over-large, and the vodka espresso martini was too sweet. This was no dampener on what was an excellent dining experience. Bookings recommended.

entry.title: Pho Nem Hanoi Home Cooking
entry.content:
Vegetable spring rolls
Pork spring rolls
Chicken with ginger rice and coleslaw
Caramelised pork belly with rice and coleslaw

Before we tear Pho Nem a new one, we’ll point out that we suspect we got a below average showing from the Hanoi Home Cooks tonight. Business Andy’s mum comes here every week, and this place was packed, so we don’t reckon they could be stuffing up this hard every night. However, we can only judge the experience that we had. And the experience that we had sucked.

Talk about wait times! We waited about 40 minutes just to get our order taken, and who knows how much longer it would have been if we hadn’t gone up to the counter to beg somebody to come and attend to us. The waiter was friendly but seemed thoroughly frazzled, as though his colleague hadn’t shown up for work and he couldn’t believe he was having to try and do all of this alone.

The spring rolls were both enjoyable, but unfortunately the mains were both sub par. The chicken had the unwelcome surprise of being on the bone. And with only chopsticks and a spoon provided to try and pull dry fried chicken off the bone? Forget about it, you’ve no choice but to eat it KFC style. We will give credit to the ginger rice though, which was the best part of the meal.

What does the word caramelised mean to you? For us it conjured images of crispy pork belly where the corners have some kind of sugar dressing that’s gone crisp and sticky. Boy were we wrong. Blubbery bits of pork belly in a nondescript brown soup where the meat was dry and the fat was smudgey. This thing was foul. The Fee-Rex had one bite and was like hell no. Business Andy, in a fit of politeness and mummys-boy-ism had said that he liked it, so ended up eating most of the meal. Sucker.

Bookings recommended
entry.summary: Vegetable spring rolls Pork spring rolls Chicken with ginger rice and coleslaw Caramelised pork belly with rice and coleslaw Before we tear Pho Nem a new one, we’ll point out that we suspect we got a below average showing from the Hanoi Home Cooks tonight. Business Andy’s mum comes here every week, and this place was packed, so we don’t reckon they could be stuffing up this hard every night. However, we can only judge the experience that we had. And the experience that we had sucked. Talk about wait times! We waited about 40 minutes just to get our order taken, and who knows how much longer it would have been if we hadn’t gone up to the counter to beg somebody to come and attend to us. The waiter was friendly but seemed thoroughly frazzled, as though his colleague hadn’t shown up for work and he couldn’t believe he was having to try and do all of this alone. The spring rolls were both enjoyable, but unfortunately the mains were both sub par. The chicken had the unwelcome surprise of being on the bone. And with only chopsticks and a spoon provided to try and pull dry fried chicken off the bone? Forget about it, you’ve no choice but to eat it KFC style. We will give credit to the ginger rice though, which was the best part of the meal. What does the word caramelised mean to you? For us it conjured images of crispy pork belly where the corners have some kind of sugar dressing that’s gone crisp and sticky. Boy were we wrong. Blubbery bits of pork belly in a nondescript brown soup where the meat was dry and the fat was smudgey. This thing was foul. The Fee-Rex had one bite and was like hell no. Business Andy, in a fit of politeness and mummys-boy-ism had said that he liked it, so ended up eating most of the meal. Sucker. Bookings recommended

entry.title: Hello Sunday
entry.content:
Mushrooms on toast
Beef burger with chips

Hello Sunday is a very popular brunch spot, and we can see why. The menu looks fab, we’d love to go back and try more

The standout was the mushrooms on toast which had this surprising little pesto swirl that really made the flavours sing. The burger was good, although the patty itself was a touch dry. Truffle mayo for the fries nom nom nom.

The cocktail menu was outstanding, we had three and they were all delicious especially the salted caramel espresso martini which we practically drank in one, we couldn’t put that thing down

Bookings recommended
entry.summary: Mushrooms on toast Beef burger with chips Hello Sunday is a very popular brunch spot, and we can see why. The menu looks fab, we’d love to go back and try more The standout was the mushrooms on toast which had this surprising little pesto swirl that really made the flavours sing. The burger was good, although the patty itself was a touch dry. Truffle mayo for the fries nom nom nom. The cocktail menu was outstanding, we had three and they were all delicious especially the salted caramel espresso martini which we practically drank in one, we couldn’t put that thing down Bookings recommended

entry.title: Tirohana Estate
entry.content:
Pear, walnut and blue cheese tart
Zucchini fritters
Lamb pot pie with mash and roast vegetables
Stuffed chicken breast with roast vegetables
Pumpkin bread and butter pudding with caramel sauce and ice cream
White chocolate berry cheesecake

If you’re looking for a fancy dining experience on a budget, look no further than Tirohana Estate. Shout out to the $72pp dinner which was three generously sized courses, plus an opening drink, amous bouche and petit fours. Definitely good value, and the food wasn’t bad at all. Nor was it perfect - the tart wasn’t very flavourful, the lamb in the pie was chewy and there were some corners of the chicken breast that were laughably dry. But then again the zucchini fritters were fun, the flavour of both mains very enjoyable, and that bread and butter pudding was downright delicious.

Tirohana Estate had friendly, genuine and attentive service. In an unusual move, the waitress read us basically the whole menu. We appreciate when a server points out menu areas of interest or importance e.g. the fish of the day is gurnard, the lamb is meant to be shared for two, we are out of the ratatouille. But this recital went on and on with no apparent point. We were also served two of the worst espresso martinis we have had in a long time. We’re pretty sure it wasn’t shaken, it had no head on it which made the three garnish coffee beans look very silly, it certainly had no actual espresso in it and I’m not sure it even had cold press. I’d wager we were looking at way too much kahlua, some vodka and a bit of simple syrup, swirled around then plonked into a cheap looking over-fat stemmed martini glass. That’s a no from us, just stick to the wine.
entry.summary: Pear, walnut and blue cheese tart Zucchini fritters Lamb pot pie with mash and roast vegetables Stuffed chicken breast with roast vegetables Pumpkin bread and butter pudding with caramel sauce and ice cream White chocolate berry cheesecake If you’re looking for a fancy dining experience on a budget, look no further than Tirohana Estate. Shout out to the $72pp dinner which was three generously sized courses, plus an opening drink, amous bouche and petit fours. Definitely good value, and the food wasn’t bad at all. Nor was it perfect - the tart wasn’t very flavourful, the lamb in the pie was chewy and there were some corners of the chicken breast that were laughably dry. But then again the zucchini fritters were fun, the flavour of both mains very enjoyable, and that bread and butter pudding was downright delicious. Tirohana Estate had friendly, genuine and attentive service. In an unusual move, the waitress read us basically the whole menu. We appreciate when a server points out menu areas of interest or importance e.g. the fish of the day is gurnard, the lamb is meant to be shared for two, we are out of the ratatouille. But this recital went on and on with no apparent point. We were also served two of the worst espresso martinis we have had in a long time. We’re pretty sure it wasn’t shaken, it had no head on it which made the three garnish coffee beans look very silly, it certainly had no actual espresso in it and I’m not sure it even had cold press. I’d wager we were looking at way too much kahlua, some vodka and a bit of simple syrup, swirled around then plonked into a cheap looking over-fat stemmed martini glass. That’s a no from us, just stick to the wine.

entry.title: Vino Vino Restaurant & Bar
entry.content:
Beef burger with coleslaw and chips
Eye fillet with chimichurri, roast potatoes, salad and portobello mushroom

Vino vino serves European fare with some South American influences. the eye fillet was a very nice piece of meat, well cooked, and the mushroom went well. The chimichurri was a non-event, a sprinkle of some green stuff which you could see but not taste, it may as well not have been there. The salad was a time-waste and the potatoes were nice but more like wedges than roast, and there weren’t many to boot.

The steak was a triumph compared to the burger. The patty was remarkable in its dryness and how little it tasted of anything. Definitely no south American flair here. We couldn’t even detect a sauce, there was no cheese, there was certainly no bacon or onion ring or anything exciting. Just a dry patty, two over-crisp buns and a sad pile of salad. The accompanying coleslaw had no flavour, but at least the chips were okay.
entry.summary: Beef burger with coleslaw and chips Eye fillet with chimichurri, roast potatoes, salad and portobello mushroom Vino vino serves European fare with some South American influences. the eye fillet was a very nice piece of meat, well cooked, and the mushroom went well. The chimichurri was a non-event, a sprinkle of some green stuff which you could see but not taste, it may as well not have been there. The salad was a time-waste and the potatoes were nice but more like wedges than roast, and there weren’t many to boot. The steak was a triumph compared to the burger. The patty was remarkable in its dryness and how little it tasted of anything. Definitely no south American flair here. We couldn’t even detect a sauce, there was no cheese, there was certainly no bacon or onion ring or anything exciting. Just a dry patty, two over-crisp buns and a sad pile of salad. The accompanying coleslaw had no flavour, but at least the chips were okay.

entry.title: Ki Maha Restaurant Waiheke Island
entry.content:
Goat cheese fritters
Duck croquettes
Olive flatbread

ki maha has lovely staff. We made Ki Maha home for a couple of hours, sipping cocktails and nibbling snacks, straddling the lunch to dinner shifts which meant we had two servers. Both were attentive, but not hovery, friendly but not overbearing, and decidedly chill.

All the food was delish, although what is the definition of a fritter? This was a fried ball of goats cheese and decidedly less flat than we were anticipating. They were also $8 each for a golf ball size, which is pretty steep. But hot damn was it good. Paired with a spicy honey we were absolutely loving it. We were also loving the flatbread which was insanely flavourful, hot, crispy, and just the right amount of oily. The least strong was the duck croquettes, which were perfectly good but didn’t blow us away.
entry.summary: Goat cheese fritters Duck croquettes Olive flatbread ki maha has lovely staff. We made Ki Maha home for a couple of hours, sipping cocktails and nibbling snacks, straddling the lunch to dinner shifts which meant we had two servers. Both were attentive, but not hovery, friendly but not overbearing, and decidedly chill. All the food was delish, although what is the definition of a fritter? This was a fried ball of goats cheese and decidedly less flat than we were anticipating. They were also $8 each for a golf ball size, which is pretty steep. But hot damn was it good. Paired with a spicy honey we were absolutely loving it. We were also loving the flatbread which was insanely flavourful, hot, crispy, and just the right amount of oily. The least strong was the duck croquettes, which were perfectly good but didn’t blow us away.

entry.title: Advieh Restaurant and Bar
entry.content:
Lamb dumpings
Carrots
Chips
Pide
Lamb neck with flatbread

Advieh serves Middle Eastern food with sophistication and flair. We loved everything. Even the chips were exceptional - super thick, hand cut, crispy, piping hot, perfectly seasoned, some kind of creamy dipping sauce, sweet heaven it was ecstasy. The dumplings were well structured and flavourful, the lamb neck absolutely falling off the bone. Every single dish was perfectly cooked and exciting.

Sounds like a 4 huh?

It would have been if not for the abysmal service. They didn’t take our order, not even a drinks order, we had to flag a guy down who had been working at a computer literally next to us, who then complained to us that we weren’t in his section. They never offered us a second round of drinks, or even refilled our water until right as we were about to leave. They forgot two of the items we ordered, and then had the gall to lie to us, saying they totally hadn’t forgotten them and just had to make more pide dough. Sorry, do you expect us to believe you made a fresh batch of yeasted dough in this time? Pull the other one mate. And to add insult to injury they never bothered to clear a single one of our plates. It’s not like they were rushed off their feet either, we were late diners and they had plenty of time to set the empty tables all around us. For shame sir, for shame.
entry.summary: Lamb dumpings Carrots Chips Pide Lamb neck with flatbread Advieh serves Middle Eastern food with sophistication and flair. We loved everything. Even the chips were exceptional - super thick, hand cut, crispy, piping hot, perfectly seasoned, some kind of creamy dipping sauce, sweet heaven it was ecstasy. The dumplings were well structured and flavourful, the lamb neck absolutely falling off the bone. Every single dish was perfectly cooked and exciting. Sounds like a 4 huh? It would have been if not for the abysmal service. They didn’t take our order, not even a drinks order, we had to flag a guy down who had been working at a computer literally next to us, who then complained to us that we weren’t in his section. They never offered us a second round of drinks, or even refilled our water until right as we were about to leave. They forgot two of the items we ordered, and then had the gall to lie to us, saying they totally hadn’t forgotten them and just had to make more pide dough. Sorry, do you expect us to believe you made a fresh batch of yeasted dough in this time? Pull the other one mate. And to add insult to injury they never bothered to clear a single one of our plates. It’s not like they were rushed off their feet either, we were late diners and they had plenty of time to set the empty tables all around us. For shame sir, for shame.

entry.title: The Pantry
entry.content:
Salted caramel apple crumble waffles
Tofu pad Thai
Baked cheesecake

Considering how busy these guys were, this food came out fast. Great service. Good food. And they had an excellent menu, especially for a brunch menu which can often be really boring. They had all sorts, there was a ramen dish, a curry, eggs benedict, it was really refreshing.

Business Andy, unfortunately, had to go waffles and then pad Thai, in a scandalous inversion of the usual savoury then sweet order of a typical meal. But it was still all enjoyable. Especially the waffles, which were delightfully fluffy, and were sweet but not cloying thanks to some thoughtful mascarpone.

The pad Thai was perfectly good, but it didn’t have something special to elevate it to a higher plane of existence. We treated ourselves to cheesecake from their breath-taking cabinet, shout out to the presentation skills of their pastry chef. Unfortunately, the baked cheesecake was served chilled, which really detracts from the texture and flavour of this dessert which should be served at room temperature. We get that they refrigerated it to preserve the very appealing presentation of cream and berries on the top, but you’ve put the cart before the horse there.
entry.summary: Salted caramel apple crumble waffles Tofu pad Thai Baked cheesecake Considering how busy these guys were, this food came out fast. Great service. Good food. And they had an excellent menu, especially for a brunch menu which can often be really boring. They had all sorts, there was a ramen dish, a curry, eggs benedict, it was really refreshing. Business Andy, unfortunately, had to go waffles and then pad Thai, in a scandalous inversion of the usual savoury then sweet order of a typical meal. But it was still all enjoyable. Especially the waffles, which were delightfully fluffy, and were sweet but not cloying thanks to some thoughtful mascarpone. The pad Thai was perfectly good, but it didn’t have something special to elevate it to a higher plane of existence. We treated ourselves to cheesecake from their breath-taking cabinet, shout out to the presentation skills of their pastry chef. Unfortunately, the baked cheesecake was served chilled, which really detracts from the texture and flavour of this dessert which should be served at room temperature. We get that they refrigerated it to preserve the very appealing presentation of cream and berries on the top, but you’ve put the cart before the horse there.

entry.title: Vietnamese Restaurant and Cafe
entry.content:
Spring rolls
Roti with peanut sauce
Popcorn chicken
Pork dumplings

It was busy in the Vietnamese Restaurant and Cafe on this Saturday afternoon. But they were clearly used to it, because our food came fast, they cleared tables fast, we watched in awe as their well oiled machine just turned over and turned over.

We’d be happy to eat everything again, except for the dumplings, which were too dumpy, and not lingy enough. Honestly they were pretty much just a ball of pork that was encased, they weren’t awful, but the innards just didn’t have a nice texture. The roti was really good, fantastically hot and fresh, crispy, with great laminations, not greasy, chef’s kiss.The popcorn chicken had an artisanal flair, nice and small but irregular in size and had a tasty dipping sauce and welcome side of peanuts. Even the salad actually was helpful to cut through the greasiness. Lastly, the spring rolls were also great. They were absolutely burning hot, so do be careful, but they had a varied filling that was very welcome and their sweet chilli sauce was thick for dipping which we appreciated.
entry.summary: Spring rolls Roti with peanut sauce Popcorn chicken Pork dumplings It was busy in the Vietnamese Restaurant and Cafe on this Saturday afternoon. But they were clearly used to it, because our food came fast, they cleared tables fast, we watched in awe as their well oiled machine just turned over and turned over. We’d be happy to eat everything again, except for the dumplings, which were too dumpy, and not lingy enough. Honestly they were pretty much just a ball of pork that was encased, they weren’t awful, but the innards just didn’t have a nice texture. The roti was really good, fantastically hot and fresh, crispy, with great laminations, not greasy, chef’s kiss.The popcorn chicken had an artisanal flair, nice and small but irregular in size and had a tasty dipping sauce and welcome side of peanuts. Even the salad actually was helpful to cut through the greasiness. Lastly, the spring rolls were also great. They were absolutely burning hot, so do be careful, but they had a varied filling that was very welcome and their sweet chilli sauce was thick for dipping which we appreciated.
